title: Quick pickled red onions
subtitle: Vinegar-pickled onions ready in an hour
excerpt: Red onions thinly sliced and left in sweetened white wine vinegar for an hour until bright pink, sharp, and crunchy. The fastest pickle and the one that goes with everything. Makes one 500 ml jar.
sourceNotes: Quick-pickled red onions are a refrigerator pickle rather than a shelf-stable preserve: the brief brine is not concentrated enough to prevent spoilage at room temperature. The technique is derived from Latin American escabeche and ceviche accompaniments and has become ubiquitous in British contemporary restaurant cooking and home cookery since the 2010s. The vinegar denatures the anthocyanin pigments in the red onion, turning them bright pink.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep refrigerated for up to two weeks. The onions soften slightly over time but remain usable.

content.text: Quick pickled red onions are not shelf-stable. They are a fridge pickle — acidic enough to keep for a fortnight in the cold but not processed for pantry storage. This makes them simple: slice, brine, wait an hour, use. The onions turn bright pink in the first 20 minutes and are ready after an hour, but their flavour deepens over several days.

Slice {{red-onion}} red onions as finely as possible into rings. Warm {{white-wine-vinegar}} of white wine vinegar in a small saucepan with {{caster-sugar}} of sugar and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt until the salt and sugar dissolve — do not boil. Pack the onion slices into a clean 500 ml jar. Pour the warm brine over the onions, pressing them down so they are submerged. Leave to cool to room temperature with the lid off, then seal and refrigerate. Ready to eat after 1 hour; best after 24 hours.

items.symptom: Onions still sharp and raw-tasting after an hour
items.cause: Not enough time, or onions cut too thick.
items.fix: Slice as thin as possible — a mandoline is ideal. The brine penetrates faster through thin slices. Leave for another hour.

items.cause: White wine vinegar more acidic than expected, or too little sugar.
items.fix: Add another half teaspoon of sugar to the brine next time. After the fact, a small pinch of sugar stirred into the jar softens the sharpness.

items.cause: Normal for the first 20 minutes — the colour develops gradually as the acid reacts with the anthocyanin.
items.fix: Leave for the full hour. If they still haven't pinked, check the onions were red (not brown-skinned) and the vinegar is acidic.

content.text: Quick pickled red onions go on almost everything. They are the standard accompaniment to pulled pork, tacos, falafel, and grain bowls. They cut through fatty or rich dishes (pork belly, lamb, smoked fish) and bring acidity to dishes that need a sharp counterpoint. A jar in the fridge is one of the most useful things you can have on standby.
